title: Education Cultures
summary: Builds the conditions for educators and educational innovation to thrive, demonstrate impact and shape the sector.
detailSummary:
Education Cultures will make King’s a place where educators are better able to lead, improve and influence education. Colleagues will have clearer opportunities to develop ideas, build confidence, access support, demonstrate impact and gain recognition for their contribution. Strong practice will be easier to surface and spread, while educational leadership and innovation will carry greater institutional esteem and external credibility.

For students, this should mean more consistently thoughtful, inclusive and evidence-informed educational experiences, shaped by educators who have the autonomy and support to improve practice. Over time, King’s will become better at learning from its own educational work, directing attention and investment towards what makes the greatest difference, and contributing a clearer and more credible voice to wider debates about education.
transformationClaim: King's has a connected, evidence-led education culture in which educators and students can shape strategic priorities, develop and test practice, demonstrate impact, gain recognition and extend King's influence.

deliverables.title: Education Culture Infrastructure
deliverables.summary: Creates the central and faculty-level infrastructure needed to strengthen education culture consistently across King’s.
deliverables.detailSummary:
Establishes the institutional infrastructure through which education culture can be convened, supported and connected more consistently across King’s. King’s Academy would provide central coordination, helping to establish shared direction, connect activity across faculties and services, support institutional conversations and ensure that educational development is linked to wider strategic priorities.

Alongside this, the deliverable would establish a proportionate expectation that every faculty has recognisable education-development capacity to support the Vice Dean Education and local educational leadership. The model would respond to faculty context rather than require identical structures everywhere. In some faculties this may build on an existing education-development unit. Elsewhere it may involve dedicated coordination or administrative support, supplemented where necessary by centrally held strategic project capacity.

The aim is to give every faculty a practical route for convening its education community, supporting colleagues to develop and share practice, connecting local priorities to institutional opportunities and ensuring that strong educational work is not dependent on informal relationships or individual goodwill. The deliverable would also clarify the respective roles of King’s Academy, faculties, professional services and institutional governance, creating a connected operating model without centralising ownership of educational practice.

deliverables.title: Strategic Communities of Practice
deliverables.summary: Creates resourced and influential communities around strategic educational priorities, giving distributed educational leadership a visible home and enabling collective expertise, innovation and evidence to shape shared institutional practice.
deliverables.detailSummary:
Strategic Communities of Practice are institutionally recognised communities that sit between strategy and educational practice. Convened around selected priorities, which might initially include areas such as experiential learning, AI in education or inclusive education, they bring together academic and professional service educators, students and partners to develop shared understanding, connect ground-up innovation and co-own educational change. They provide a locus of energy and influence for educational leadership, while helping King’s develop greater collective resonance without imposing uniform solutions from the centre.

Each community combines a committed core with a wider, porous periphery, while agile shards undertake focused work around particular challenges or opportunities. Through events, collaborative inquiry, commissioned activity, seed funding, showcases and wider outreach, communities engage colleagues beyond existing enthusiasts, test and evidence emerging ideas, and help effective practice travel across the institution. Workloaded conveners sustain the community, while an Innovation Subcommittee provides strategic direction, a commissioning budget and light-touch accountability. Together, this creates a practical mechanism through which distinctive King’s provisions, practices and pedagogies can emerge from distributed expertise and genuine co-construction.

caseForChange.problem:
King’s has substantial educational expertise, leadership and innovation distributed across faculties, professional services and student communities. Much of this work is thoughtful and ambitious, but it can remain locally bounded, weakly connected to related activity elsewhere and only loosely linked to institutional priorities. Promising ideas are therefore harder to discover, similar challenges may be addressed repeatedly, and opportunities for collective learning and wider influence are missed.

King’s also faces a more complex challenge of coherence. The university increasingly needs to identify common points of resonance across its educational provision, while preserving disciplinary difference, local agency and professional judgement. Central policy and standardisation can create consistency, but can also become overly directive and generate compliance rather than shared ownership. King’s currently lacks a sufficiently influential middle layer through which common approaches can be developed collaboratively from practice.

Educational leadership remains comparatively minoritised within academic life. Relative to research, there are fewer visible and resourced spaces in which colleagues can build identity, invest collective energy and exercise influence beyond their immediate context. Educational innovation can also be supported financially without a consistent means of connecting activity, governing the wider portfolio or learning collectively from the investment.
caseForChange.opportunity:
Strategic Communities of Practice offer King’s a mechanism for distributed educational leadership. They create recognised spaces in which educators, students, professional services and partners can interpret shared challenges, connect local expertise and shape institutional responses together.

Sitting between strategy and practice, the communities can identify areas of common resonance, surface and connect promising work, convene sustained educational conversations, stimulate innovation and generate evidence for wider development. They provide a home for those already leading educational change, while deliberately reaching out to involve a broader institutional constituency.

Their combination of distributed participation, delegated resource and strategic governance allows King’s to develop greater coherence without central prescription. Shared practices can emerge through inquiry, experimentation and co-construction, with the legitimacy and collective ownership needed for them to travel.
caseForChange.whyNow:
King’s is renewing its focus on education as a field of academic leadership and on the development of credible, rewarding education-focused career pathways. This creates an opportunity to give colleagues a stronger locus for educational contribution, identity and influence, and to connect individual development with wider institutional improvement.

The university is also seeking to add more deliberate value to educational practice in ways that enhance student outcomes and experiences. The Education Delivery Framework establishes a number of common themes around which provision across King’s can find greater collective resonance. These themes now need spaces in which they can be interpreted, challenged and developed by the people who teach, support and learn at King’s.

At the same time, King’s is considering more distinctive provisions, practices and pedagogies in emerging areas. Establishing Strategic Communities of Practice now provides a way to develop these collaboratively, rather than moving too quickly from institutional ambition to centrally determined solutions.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
King’s develops a repeatable model through which strategic educational priorities are shaped by distributed leadership and collective expertise.

Colleagues gain visible, resourced and influential spaces in which to develop their practice, contribute beyond their immediate setting and shape wider educational direction. Ground-up innovation becomes easier to identify, connect, support and learn from, while institutional investment is more coherently stewarded.

The Education Delivery Framework becomes a basis for shared inquiry and development rather than a mechanism for uniformity. Common provisions, practices and pedagogies emerge where they add value, while remaining responsive to disciplinary context, local expertise and student experience.

Over time, King’s becomes better able to combine institutional coherence with local agency. Educational leadership gains greater visibility and esteem, promising practice travels more readily, and educational change is more genuinely shared, evidence-informed and enduring.

deliverables.title: Educational Impact, Evidence and Recognition
deliverables.summary: Builds a shared approach to evidencing educational impact and using that evidence to strengthen recognition, progression and esteem.
deliverables.detailSummary:
Creates a shared institutional approach to understanding the impact of educational work on students and the health of education culture at King’s. It would bring together measures that already exist across the institution into a more coherent evaluation framework, helping colleagues ask what works, for whom, under what conditions and with what effect on student experience and outcomes.

The primary focus would be student impact, including satisfaction, academic achievement, career readiness, destinations, belonging, self-efficacy, wellbeing and engagement. Attendance and KEATS activity would provide important measures of institutional engagement, alongside broader participation in learning, services, enrichment and enhancement. Equity of impact would run through the framework, ensuring that aggregate improvement does not conceal uneven experience or outcomes between student groups.

The framework would be proportionate to the scale of the work being evaluated. Small exploratory interventions should be able to use light-touch methods, while larger institutional initiatives should be supported to develop stronger evaluation designs, baselines and comparative evidence. A central education impact and evaluation capability would help educators and projects use institutional data responsibly, develop theories of impact and make informed decisions about what should grow, change or stop.

Alongside student impact, the deliverable would establish a light-touch account of education culture itself. This could include patterns in education-focused promotion, participation in development opportunities and strategic communities, applications for innovation support, educational leadership activity and external engagement.

This stronger evidence base would also support recognition. Educators would have clearer ways to evidence their contribution and impact for progression, promotion and institutional recognition. This would include colleagues on academic education pathways and professional services educators whose directly educative work is often significant but less consistently visible. The deliverable would help turn educational contribution into credible narratives of leadership, impact and esteem, supported by practical opportunities such as recognised leadership roles, project secondments, bought-out time and development support.

deliverables.title: Innovation Incubation and External Influence
deliverables.summary: Creates a supported route through which promising educational ideas can be tested, developed, scaled and projected externally.
deliverables.detailSummary:
Establishes a coherent incubation model for educational innovation, giving promising ideas access to the time, resource, expertise and support needed to move from early exploration into credible practice. It would provide a visible route through which ideas emerging from faculties, services, Strategic Communities of Practice and student collaboration can be identified, tested, evaluated and developed.

The model would operate at more than one level. Small seed investments would support rapid experimentation, exploratory work and ideas that are not yet ready for substantial institutional commitment. A smaller number of strategic flagship interventions would receive more sustained support, stronger evaluation and clearer routes to institutional adoption or external profile. The approach would be open to innovation within the curriculum and in more agile co-curricular or extracurricular settings, including King’s Edge where appropriate.

Incubation would involve more than funding. It would connect innovators to learning design, project management, evaluation, data, student co-creation, partnership development and institutional decision routes. Promising practice would be supported to clarify the problem it addresses, test its assumptions, understand its impact and determine whether it should be scaled, embedded, adapted or concluded. Existing innovation funds could gradually be connected through this common framework, making investment more visible, strategically aligned and consistently evaluated.

The deliverable would also create deliberate routes for externalisation. King’s Academy would work with educators, communities and projects to turn credible practice and evidence into sector-facing outputs, including articles, reports, blogs, podcasts, conference contributions, partnerships, events and public conversations. The Policy Institute would act as a standing partner, helping connect educational practice to policy thinking and translate evaluated institutional experience into sector and government-facing influence.

The ambition is for King’s to build an external voice grounded in implemented practice and credible evidence. Educational innovation would therefore contribute not only to internal improvement, but also to institutional reputation, sector leadership and wider policy influence.

title: Curriculum Framework and Review
summary: Provides the route through which King's Edge requirements are embedded in curriculum audit, review and enhancement.
edgeRole: Carries the curriculum audit and review mechanism that surfaces skills, experiential learning, graduate premium contribution and growth priorities.

title: Single Student App / Digital Portal
summary: Provides the digital environment through which the King's Edge offer becomes navigable, personal and usable for students.
edgeRole: Carries the digital implementation for King's Canvas, opportunity navigation, evidence capture, digital badges, microcredentials and the future enhanced student record.
